manifest.name: code-review
manifest.title: Code Review Research Plan
manifest.subtitle: Analyzing the Mind of Code Reviewers Across Top 11 Open Source Repositories
manifest.home-description: Analyzing review strategies across top 11 open source repositories

key-box.heading: Objective
key-box.paragraph: This research plan aims to decode the cognitive frameworks and methodological approaches used by code reviewers across the world's most influential open source repositories. By analyzing 11 top-tier projects, we identify universal review patterns, architecture evaluation strategies, and design assessment techniques that define excellence in code review.

card.heading: Strategy Analysis
card.paragraph: What systematic approaches do reviewers use to evaluate code changes? How do they balance thoroughness with velocity?

card.heading: Architecture Assessment
card.paragraph: How do reviewers evaluate architectural impact? What patterns indicate good vs. poor architectural decisions?

card.heading: Design Evaluation
card.paragraph: What design principles consistently emerge? How do reviewers assess code maintainability and extensibility?

card.heading: Quality Gates
card.paragraph: What automated and manual checks form the quality barriers? How do projects define "ready to merge"?

section.paragraph: Every code reviewer operates within a mental model that spans five core dimensions:

row.cell: Does it work? Edge cases? Error handling?
Fits design? No tech debt? Scalable?

section.paragraph: Across all 11 repositories, code review follows a consistent five-phase progression:

row.cell: Understanding the problem, reading related issues, examining existing code patterns, and understanding the contributor's intent.
Evaluating approach validity, architecture fit, and whether the solution aligns with project goals and patterns.
Line-by-line examination, logic verification, style compliance, edge case analysis, and test evaluation.
Assessing how the change integrates with existing systems, potential conflicts, and downstream effects.
Approve, request changes, or reject based on accumulated evidence and project standards.

list.item: Code compiles and passes all automated tests
Follows established coding style and conventions
Includes appropriate tests (unit, integration, e2e)
Documentation is updated (code comments, README, API docs)
Commit messages are descriptive and follow project format
No security vulnerabilities introduced
Performance implications are considered and documented
Backward compatibility is maintained or explicitly broken with migration path
Error handling is comprehensive and graceful
Resource management (memory, files, connections) is proper
Large, monolithic PRs: Changes spanning too many files or concerns
Tight coupling: Creating unnecessary dependencies between unrelated modules
Copy-paste code: Duplicating logic instead of abstracting
Magic numbers/strings: Hardcoded values without explanation or constants
Premature optimization: Complex code for theoretical performance gains
Over-engineering: Building for hypothetical future requirements
Breaking encapsulation: Reaching into private/internal APIs
Ignoring error cases: Happy-path-only implementation

paragraph.strong: C | 30M+ LOC | Mailing List Based | 25+ Years

list.item: Email-based workflow: Patches submitted via git send-email to subsystem mailing lists
Subsystem maintainers: Hierarchical review through MAINTAINERS file and get_maintainer.pl script
Developer Certificate of Origin: Every patch must include Signed-off-by chain
Review tags: Formal Reviewed-by, Acked-by, Tested-by tags with legal meaning
Single logical change per patch: Strict separation of concerns; mixed changes rejected
User-visible impact must be described (crashes, lockups, performance regressions)
Quantified optimizations with trade-off analysis required
Internal API changes must be justified with impact assessment
Build verification after each patch in series (for bisectability)

paragraph.strong: JavaScript/TypeScript | UI Library | GitHub PRs | Component-Based

list.item: Component-centric thinking: Reviews focus on component boundaries, props contracts, and state ownership
Declarative UI patterns: Emphasis on "Thinking in React" - breaking UI into component hierarchy
One-way data flow enforcement: Reviewers verify unidirectional data flow and proper state lifting
Hook rules compliance: Strict enforcement of Rules of Hooks (call order, conditional hooks)
Component decomposition following single responsibility principle
Props vs State determination (DRY principle for state)
Inverse data flow patterns (callback props for child-to-parent communication)
Reconciliation efficiency and key prop correctness
Effect dependencies completeness in useEffect hooks

paragraph.strong: Go | Container Orchestration | GitHub PRs | SIG-Based Governance

list.item: SIG (Special Interest Group) ownership: Different SIGs own different parts; PRs must respect boundaries
Enhancement Proposal Process (KEP): Large changes require formal proposal before code review
OWNERS file system: Automated reviewer assignment based on code ownership
Prow bot automation: /lgtm, /approve, /retest commands for review workflow
Strict size limits: "Smaller is Better" philosophy; large PRs actively discouraged
KISS, YAGNI, MVP principles explicitly enforced
API conventions compliance (naming, versioning, backward compatibility)
No cross-repository PRs; changes must be scoped to single SIG when possible
E2E test requirements for all functional changes
Release note requirements for user-facing changes

paragraph.strong: TypeScript | Code Editor | GitHub PRs | Electron

list.item: Issue-first approach: Significant changes must be tied to issues; "help wanted" labels guide contributors
Performance-first culture: "Lightweight" is a core value; performance regression risks rejected
UX team involvement: UI changes must go through UX review and feature owner approval
Electron API restrictions: New Electron APIs require unit tests in Electron upstream
Architecture approval: Team must agree on architectural impact before merging
Multi-process architecture awareness (renderer, extension host, search processes)
Web standards preference over Electron APIs (for web client compatibility)
Extension API design requires discussion and agreement
Platform abstraction (Windows, macOS, Linux, Web)
Memory and startup performance impact assessment
Gerrit-based workflow: Not GitHub PRs; uses git cl upload to Gerrit instance
Design documents required: Nontrivial efforts (>1 person-month) need formal design docs
Committers vs non-committers: Different approval requirements (1 vs 2 Code-Review +1)
OWNERS file hierarchy: Every affected file must have owner approval
Commit Queue (CQ): Automated testing across multiple platforms before merge
Code must serve other Chromium code (no dead code)
Centralization only when multiple consumers benefit
Refactoring existing code to fit new features properly
80% rule: Team mandating change does 80% of migration work
New language/toolchain additions require ATL_OWNERS approval
AI coding assistant disclosure required

paragraph.strong: Python | Web Framework | Trac + GitHub | BDFL Model

list.item: Trac ticket requirement: Non-trivial PRs without Trac tickets are closed automatically
Documentation-first: Extensive contribution guidelines in official docs
BDFL model: Final decisions rest with technical board/core devs
Backwards compatibility: Strict deprecation policy; breaking changes require major releases
Code of Conduct enforcement: Strong community standards
ORM query optimization and database backend neutrality
Middleware and request/response cycle integrity
Template system security (auto-escaping, XSS prevention)
Settings system design (lazy evaluation, override behavior)
App encapsulation and reusable app design patterns

paragraph.strong: Rust | Programming Language | GitHub PRs | RFC Process

list.item: RFC (Request for Comments) process: Substantial changes require formal RFC before implementation
rustc-dev-guide: Extensive compiler architecture documentation reviewers expect familiarity with
Subtrees and submodules: Changes must be directed to correct repository in ecosystem
Zulip community: Real-time discussion encouraged before PR submission
No merge commits: Linear history enforced through rebasing
Type system soundness and borrow checker correctness
MIR (Mid-level IR) and LLVM IR generation correctness
Standard library API design (ergonomics, zero-cost abstractions)
Compiler pass ordering and optimization correctness
Cross-platform ABI compliance

paragraph.strong: JavaScript/C++ | Runtime | GitHub PRs | Open Governance

list.item: Open governance model: Collaborators elected based on contributions; no corporate control
Large PR guidelines: Special process for changes >500 lines
Bot authorization: Unauthorized automation subject to immediate moderation
CLA and DCO: Developer's Certificate of Origin required
Multiple review rounds: Explicit "please take another look" (PTAL) culture
libuv integration and event loop impact
V8 API compatibility and wrapper object correctness
Native addon ABI stability (N-API vs NAN)
Platform abstraction (Windows, POSIX, libuv)
Memory management in C++ bindings

paragraph.strong: Scala/Java/Python | Big Data | GitHub PRs | Apache Way

list.item: JIRA integration: Changes must reference JIRA issues; no standalone PRs
Apache license compliance: Original work affirmation required
Third-party project criteria: New features evaluated if they can stand alone
Community consensus: Changes must be "important and ready enough" for community time
Clear motivation requirement: Changes must be clearly explained and motivated
Distributed computing correctness (shuffle, partitioning, serialization)
DataFrame/Dataset API design and optimization
Catalyst optimizer rule correctness
Cross-language API parity (Scala, Java, Python, R)
Memory management and spill-to-disk behavior

paragraph.strong: Python/C++ | ML Framework | GitHub PRs | Google-Managed

list.item: Contributor License Agreement: Google CLA required for all contributions
SIG-focused: Special Interest Groups for different domains (SIG Build, SIG MLIR, etc.)
API review process: Public API changes require additional scrutiny
Testing rigor: Extensive test requirements including GPU and distributed tests
Performance benchmarks: Regressions must be justified with benchmark data
Graph execution and eager execution compatibility
XLA compilation and device placement correctness
Op kernel implementation (CPU, GPU, TPU variants)
Gradient correctness in automatic differentiation
Model serialization (SavedModel) backward compatibility

paragraph.strong: Go | Programming Language + Stdlib | Gerrit | Google-Managed

list.item: Gerrit-based workflow: Uses git-codereview tool; changes sent to Gerrit, not GitHub PRs
Single-commit branches: Each change must be a single commit per branch; squashing enforced
Thorough review culture: "Think of each review comment like a ticket" - every comment must be addressed
Google employee requirement: Two Google employees must be involved (uploader or +1 reviewer) for compliance
Proposal process: Significant language/library/tool changes require formal proposal before code review
Gopherbot bridge: GitHub PRs automatically synced to Gerrit for first-time contributors
Go idioms and simplicity ("less is more" philosophy)
Backward compatibility for stdlib APIs (Go 1 compatibility promise)
Compiler and runtime correctness (especially GC, scheduler, goroutine semantics)
Cross-architecture correctness (trybots test multiple architectures automatically)
Performance: benchstat tool used for benchmark data in commit messages
go fmt enforcement - style violations rejected at commit time

key-box.heading: Used by: Linux Kernel, Chromium, React
key-box.paragraph: Reviewers start with the user-visible impact, API surface, and architecture fit before diving into implementation details. This prevents wasting time on well-implemented solutions to wrong problems.

list.item: Read PR description and linked issues
Examine public API changes
Verify approach aligns with project architecture
Check test coverage at high level
Then review implementation details

key-box.heading: Used by: Kubernetes, VS Code, Chromium
key-box.paragraph: Projects maintain explicit checklists that reviewers follow systematically, ensuring no dimension is overlooked.

list.item: Code compiles and tests pass locally
Style guide compliance verified
OWNERS approval obtained for all affected files
Bug reference included in commit message
Manual testing described if not covered by trybots
Security implications considered
Privacy implications considered
Internationalization considerations addressed

key-box.heading: Used by: Rust, Node.js, Django
key-box.paragraph: Different reviewers focus on different layers: syntax/style, logic correctness, architecture, performance, security.

row.cell: Linters, formatters, static analysis, test suites run by CI
Edge cases, error handling, algorithm correctness
How change fits with rest of system, migration paths

key-box.heading: Used by: Rust, Django, Node.js
key-box.paragraph: Reviewers treat code review as a mentoring opportunity, explaining not just what to change but why, fostering community knowledge.

list.item: Explaining idiomatic patterns in the language/framework
Suggesting design alternatives with trade-offs
Linking to relevant documentation or prior discussions
Citing specific style guide sections
Providing context on historical decisions

section.paragraph: Reviewers evaluate what constraints inform the design decision:

list.item: What products depend on this code?
What platforms must it support?
What performance requirements exist?
What security boundaries must be respected?

paragraph.em: "Code in the Chromium project should be in service of other code in the Chromium project."

section.paragraph: Reviewers ask: Should existing code be refactored first?

list.item: Is the existing code designed for this extension?
Should prefactoring happen in a separate PR?
Is technical debt being accumulated?

paragraph.em: "The code likely wasn't designed for everything we are trying to do with it now. Take time to refactor."

list.item: Can this be made more generic and moved up?
Is this premature abstraction?
Does it duplicate existing patterns?
Is it YAGNI (You Aren't Gonna Need It)?
Is the API surface minimal and complete?
Are naming conventions consistent?
Is backward compatibility maintained?
Are error messages actionable?
Is the API ergonomic for users?

row.cell: Method accessing data from another object more than its own
Module A depends on B which depends on A
Abstractions for future requirements that may never come

list.item: Study each repository's CONTRIBUTING.md and development process documentation
Map the review workflow (mailing list, GitHub PR, Gerrit, etc.)
Identify key reviewers and maintainers in each project
Catalog the automated checks and CI/CD pipelines
Workflow comparison matrix
Key personnel directory
Automation inventory
Analyze 50+ merged PRs from each repository
Document reviewer comments and feedback categories
Identify common approval criteria and rejection reasons
Map the evolution of PRs through multiple review rounds
Comment taxonomy (types of feedback reviewers give)
Approval criteria checklist per project
Review round analysis (average iterations, time to merge)
Study design documents and RFCs from each project
Interview core maintainers about their review philosophy
Analyze architectural decision records (ADRs)
Map the relationship between design docs and code review
Architecture review rubric
Maintainer interview summaries
Decision pattern catalog
Compare effectiveness of different review models
Identify which practices correlate with project health metrics
Develop a universal code review framework
Create actionable recommendations for teams
Comparative effectiveness report
Universal code review framework
Team adoption guide
Final research report with recommendations
Present findings to selected project maintainers for validation
Test framework adoption with volunteer teams
Gather feedback and iterate

key-box.heading: The Universal Review Formula
key-box.paragraph: Across all 11 repositories, effective code review follows this pattern:

list.item: Context before code: Understand the problem before evaluating the solution
Architecture before implementation: Validate approach before reviewing style
Automation before manual: Let CI catch trivial issues; humans focus on design
Teaching before enforcing: Explain the "why" to build community knowledge
Integration before isolation: Consider how changes affect the whole system

card.heading: For Small Teams
card.paragraph: Adopt React's component-boundary thinking and Linux's "single logical change" principle. Focus on teaching-oriented reviews.

card.heading: For Enterprise Teams
card.paragraph: Use Chromium's design doc requirement for large changes and Kubernetes' OWNERS file approach for code ownership.

card.heading: For Open Source Projects
card.paragraph: Emulate Node.js's open governance or Django's ticket-first approach. Document your process clearly.

card.heading: For Performance-Critical Code
card.paragraph: Adopt VS Code's performance-first culture and TensorFlow's benchmark requirements. Quantify impact.

syllabus.footer-text: Code Review Research Plan — Analysis of Top 11 Open Source Repositories
